item.title: Creamy Chicken and Broccoli Casserole: Easy Weeknight Dinner
item.description: Creamy chicken and broccoli casserole that's ready in just 30 minutes! Loaded with tender chicken and vibrant broccoli in a rich cream sauce. Great for busy weeknights or cozy family dinners. Save this recipe for later! | ⏱️ Prep: 15min | Cook: 30min | Serves: 4 | #cat1761230383494 #Easypost #1hourmeals #post #cooking
item.dc:creator: author_001
item.content:encoded:
⏱️ Prep Time: 15 minutes

🍳 Cook Time: 30 minutes

⏰ Total Time: N/A minutes

🍽️ Servings: 4

📊 Difficulty: Easy

🥘 Ingredients

  • 2 cups cooked chicken, shredded
  • 3 cups broccoli florets, chopped
  • 1 can (10.5 oz) cream of chicken soup
  • 1 cup sour cream
  • 1 cup shredded cheddar cheese (I use Kerrygold)
  • 1 teaspoon garlic powder
  • 1 teaspoon onion powder
  • Salt and pepper to taste
  • 2 cups cooked rice
  • 1/2 cup breadcrumbs (optional for topping)

👩‍🍳 Instructions

  1. Preheat your oven to 350°F (175°C). While it's heating, grab a large mixing bowl and combine the shredded chicken, broccoli, cream of chicken soup, sour cream, and half the cheese. Season with garlic powder, onion powder, salt, and pepper. Mix until everything is well combined.
  2. In a separate pot, cook your rice according to package instructions. This step usually takes about 15 minutes. Drain and set aside when done.
  3. Once the oven's ready, fold the cooked rice into the chicken mixture until everything is evenly coated. Spread the mixture into a greased casserole dish.
  4. Top it off with the remaining cheese and breadcrumbs if using. This adds a nice crunch that everyone loves.
  5. Bake in the preheated oven for about 25-30 minutes, or until the cheese is bubbly and golden brown on top. You’ll know it's done when it starts smelling amazing.
  6. Let it cool for a few minutes before serving. I like to sprinkle some fresh herbs on top for a pop of color.

item.title: Creamy Garlic Mashed Potatoes: Building Texture and Emulsion
item.description: A guide to making mashed potatoes that achieve creamy texture and balanced garlic flavor through proper emulsification and heat management. Learn why Yukon gold potatoes work better than russets, how starch and fat interact, and how to prevent gluey, broken texture. | ⏱️ Prep: 15min | Cook: 20min | Serves: 4 | #cat1761230294638 #Easypost #1hourmeals #post #cooking
item.dc:creator: author_001
item.content:encoded:
⏱️ Prep Time: 15 minutes

🍳 Cook Time: 20 minutes

⏰ Total Time: 35 minutes

🍽️ Servings: 4

📊 Difficulty: Easy

🥘 Ingredients

  • 2 pounds (900g) Yukon gold potatoes, peeled and quartered
  • 1/2 teaspoon (3g) fine sea salt, for boiling water
  • 6 tablespoons (85g) unsalted butter, divided
  • 4 cloves (20g) garlic, minced
  • 1/2 cup (120ml) heavy cream, warmed to room temperature
  • 1/4 teaspoon (0.5g) white pepper, or black pepper to taste
  • 1/4 teaspoon (0.5g) fine sea salt, for seasoning (adjust to taste)
  • Optional garnish: 1 tablespoon (3g) fresh chives, finely chopped

👩‍🍳 Instructions

  1. 1 Fill a large pot with approximately 2 quarts (1.9 liters) of cold water and add 1/2 teaspoon (3g) of salt. The salt should dissolve completely. Bring the water to a rolling boil, which takes approximately 8-10 minutes with a covered pot. While waiting for the water to boil, peel the Yukon gold potatoes using a vegetable peeler or paring knife. Cut them into quarters that are roughly equal in size, approximately 2-3 inches (5-7 cm) across. Uniform sizing ensures even cooking—smaller pieces will become mushy while larger pieces remain undercooked if sizes vary too much. 8-10 minutes for water to boil, 5 minutes for preparation Water reaches rolling boil with large bubbles bursting aggressively at the surface, potatoes are cut into uniform quarter-sized pieces
  2. 2 Once the water reaches a rolling boil, carefully add all the potato pieces. The water will temporarily drop in temperature as the cold potatoes are added. Maintain a gentle boil (not a rolling boil) by adjusting the heat to medium-high. The potatoes will be fully submerged as they sink. Set a timer for 15-18 minutes. The exact time depends on the potatoes' size and your stove's heat, but do not cook longer than 18 minutes—overcooked potatoes absorb excess water and become gluey when mashed. 15-18 minutes Potatoes are fork-tender and offer zero resistance when pierced with a knife, potato skin begins to visibly separate in places
  3. 3 While the potatoes cook, prepare the garlic and butter. Mince 4 cloves of garlic into small, uniform pieces approximately 1/8 inch (3mm) in size. Place a small saucepan over low heat and add 2 tablespoons (28g) of butter. Allow the butter to melt slowly without browning. Once melted, add the minced garlic and stir constantly for 1-2 minutes. The garlic should release its aroma and become slightly softened but never brown or crispy. Brown garlic tastes acrid and bitter. Remove from heat and set aside. Do not combine the garlic butter mixture with the remaining butter—keep it separate. 3-4 minutes Butter is fully melted and foamy, garlic is fragrant with no browning visible, mixture is golden and clear
  4. 4 Once the potatoes are fork-tender, immediately drain them in a colander but do not rinse them. Rinsing removes starch from the surface, which is essential for proper mashing texture. Reserve approximately 1/4 cup (60ml) of the cooking water in case the final mash needs thinning. Return the hot, drained potatoes to the warm pot. The residual heat will continue softening the potatoes slightly and will help the butter and cream integrate better. 2-3 minutes Potatoes are in the pot, steam is rising, pot is warm to the touch
  5. 5 Before adding any butter or cream, allow the potatoes to rest for 1-2 minutes to release some of their steam. This prevents excess moisture from creating gluey texture. Then, begin mashing using a potato masher, working from the bottom of the pot upward. Use steady, firm pressure rather than aggressive pounding. Mash for approximately 2-3 minutes until potatoes are mostly broken down but still contain some small lumps. You should not see large chunks, but a few small specks of potato (approximately the size of peas) are fine and contribute to texture. 3-4 minutes Potatoes are mostly smooth with texture like coarse sand, no large chunks remain, steam is rising steadily
  6. 6 Add the remaining 4 tablespoons (56g) of unsalted butter in two additions, stirring well after each addition. The butter should integrate quickly and create a glossy sheen. Then add the garlic-infused butter mixture and stir thoroughly. The heat from the potatoes will warm everything to the same temperature, promoting proper emulsification. 1-2 minutes Butter is fully integrated and creates glossy appearance, no visible butter streaks remain, mixture is cohesive
  7. 7 Pour the room-temperature cream into the pot in a thin stream while stirring constantly. Do not add all the cream at once. The steady stirring and gradual addition allow the cream to emulsify properly with the starch and fat. The potatoes will immediately become creamier and smoother as the cream incorporates. Continue stirring for 1-2 minutes after all cream is added until the texture is completely uniform and smooth. 2-3 minutes Cream is fully incorporated, texture is uniform and creamy throughout, no streaks of cream or butter visible
  8. 8 Taste the potatoes and season with white pepper (which is milder and less visible than black pepper) and additional salt as needed. Start with 1/4 teaspoon (0.5g) of salt and add incrementally, tasting after each addition. The potatoes should taste seasoned but not salty. The garlic should be apparent as a savory depth, not a sharp, biting flavor. If the mixture seems too thick, thin it with reserved potato cooking water, adding 1-2 tablespoons (15-30ml) at a time. 2-3 minutes Potatoes taste properly seasoned, flavors are well-developed and balanced, texture is creamy but not overly thick
  9. 9 Transfer to a serving dish immediately and garnish with chopped fresh chives if desired. Mashed potatoes are best served hot, within 15 minutes of finishing. If holding for longer periods, transfer to a warm oven (200°F / 93°C) and stir occasionally, but this changes the texture slightly over time as the fat separates. 5 minutes Potatoes are in serving dish, steam is rising, dish is warm to the touch

item.title: Brown Butter Mashed Potatoes: Building Creaminess and Managing Starch Breakdown
item.description: A guide to making silky mashed potatoes with brown butter that achieves perfect creaminess without becoming gluey. Learn why potato variety matters, how to manage starch breakdown to preserve texture, and the chemistry behind brown butter emulsification. | ⏱️ Prep: 15min | Cook: 25min | Serves: 6 | #cat1761230636499 #Easypost #1hourmeals #post #cooking
item.dc:creator: author_001
item.content:encoded:
⏱️ Prep Time: 15 minutes

🍳 Cook Time: 25 minutes

⏰ Total Time: 40 minutes

🍽️ Servings: 6

📊 Difficulty: Easy

🥘 Ingredients

  • 2.5 lbs (1.1kg) russet potatoes, peeled and cut into 1.5-inch (4cm) cubes
  • 1 tsp (6g) fine sea salt, divided
  • 1/2 cup (115g) unsalted butter
  • 1/2 cup (120ml) heavy cream, warmed
  • 1/4 cup (60ml) whole milk, warmed
  • 1/8 tsp (0.5g) white pepper
  • 1/4 tsp (0.5g) freshly grated nutmeg
  • 2 tbsp (6g) fresh chives, finely chopped, for garnish

👩‍🍳 Instructions

  1. 1 Peel the potatoes and cut them into 1.5-inch (4cm) cubes. Place them in a large pot and cover with cold water by about 2 inches (5cm). This starting with cold water rather than hot water allows the potatoes to heat gradually, which helps them cook evenly. Add 1 teaspoon (6g) of fine sea salt to the water. The salt should dissolve into the water, making the water taste like seawater (salty but not inedible). This salt penetrates the potato cells during cooking, seasoning them from the inside. Place the pot over high heat and bring the water to a boil, uncovered. 5-7 minutes to reach boil Water is at a rolling boil with vigorous bubbling, steam rises from the pot, water is cloudy white (from released potato starch)
  2. 2 Once the water reaches a boil, adjust the heat to maintain a gentle boil (not a rolling boil). A gentle boil is characterized by steady bubbling rather than vigorous, aggressive bubbling. Boil the potatoes uncovered for approximately 15-20 minutes, depending on the potato cubes' exact size and the specific potato variety. Russet potatoes usually cook in this timeframe. Begin testing for doneness at 15 minutes by piercing a potato piece with a fork—the fork should slide through the potato with no resistance. If you feel resistance or if the potato is still firm, continue cooking and check again in 2-3 minutes. 15-20 minutes Fork slides through potato pieces with zero resistance, potatoes are soft and beginning to fall slightly apart, cooking water is now quite cloudy white from released starch
  3. 3 Place a colander in your sink and carefully drain the potatoes by pouring the contents of the pot into the colander. Allow the potatoes to drain for about 30 seconds, pressing gently with the back of a spoon to release excess water without mashing the potatoes. The potatoes will still be hot, which is correct—you want to work with warm potatoes. Do not rinse the drained potatoes with cold water, which some posts recommend. Rinsing removes the starch coating on the potatoes, and that starch is what helps create the creamy texture through emulsification. 1 minute Most excess water has drained from the colander, potatoes no longer drip water, potatoes are still steaming hot to the touch
  4. 4 Return the drained potatoes to the hot pot. Place the pot over low heat (or remove the pot from heat entirely if your stovetop has residual heat). Let the potatoes sit in the pot for approximately 1 minute. You'll notice the potatoes visibly dry out and the color becomes lighter and less translucent. This brief resting period allows residual heat to evaporate surface moisture clinging to the potato pieces. This step is crucial because it prevents excess water from being incorporated into the mashed potatoes. 1-2 minutes Potatoes appear drier, surface moisture has evaporated, potatoes are lighter in appearance, small wisps of steam rise from them
  5. 5 Begin mashing the potatoes gently using a potato masher, pressing the potatoes against the side of the pot. Use gentle pressure and folding motions rather than vigorous mashing or stirring. Push the masher down and slightly to the side, folding the partly-mashed potatoes over the unmashed pieces. Repeat this motion, rotating to different areas of the pot, until the potatoes reach your desired texture. For most preferences, the potatoes should be mostly smooth but with no visible large lumps of unmashed potato. The process should take approximately 1-2 minutes. Avoid over-working the potatoes—excessive mashing ruptures the starch granules and releases starch into the liquid, creating a gluey texture. 1-2 minutes Potatoes are mostly smooth with only tiny lumps visible, no large unbroken pieces remain, texture is light and fluffy appearing, potatoes still steam
  6. 6 While the potatoes are mashing, begin browning the butter in a small saucepan over medium heat. Add the 1/2 cup (115g) of unsalted butter and watch it carefully. The butter will first melt, appearing as clear, golden liquid. Then it will begin to foam as water in the butter evaporates. Continue heating, swirling the pan occasionally (don't stir with a spoon, just tilt and swirl the pan gently). After approximately 3-5 minutes, the foaming will subside and the butter will be clear again. Around 4-6 minutes total, the milk solids (which have sunk to the bottom of the pan) will begin to brown. You'll notice the butter darkens to a light golden color, then progresses to a deeper golden-brown. At the same time, an aroma develops—it will smell toasty, nutty, and pleasant. As soon as you see the milk solids are deep golden-brown (about the color of caramel), remove the pan from heat. The butter should smell intensely nutty, not burnt or bitter. This is the critical moment—over-heat even slightly and the butter tastes burnt rather than nutty. 5-7 minutes Butter has changed from clear golden to deep golden-brown, aroma is intensely nutty and toasty (not burnt), milk solids visible at bottom are deep brown, butter is foaming has subsided to calm surface
  7. 7 Immediately pour the brown butter and its milk solids (pour carefully so the solids come along) from the saucepan into the mashed potatoes. The brown butter should be added while it's still hot, which ensures the potatoes remain warm enough for the starch to properly emulsify the liquid. Do not pour the brown butter gently and slowly—pour it in one motion into the potatoes. Then, using a wooden spoon or spatula, fold the brown butter into the potatoes using gentle folding motions, turning the potatoes over themselves and mixing until the butter is distributed throughout. This folding process should take about 30-45 seconds. Avoid stirring vigorously, which would over-work the potatoes. 1 minute Brown butter is distributed throughout potatoes, no streaks of butter remain visible, potatoes have a light golden color from the brown butter, mixture appears silky
  8. 8 Warm the heavy cream and whole milk together—you can do this while the butter is browning by placing both in a small saucepan over low heat or by microwaving them together in a small microwave-safe bowl for 60-90 seconds. The cream and milk should be warm to the touch (approximately 120-130°F / 49-54°C) but not hot or steaming. Pour the warmed cream and milk into the mashed potatoes and fold gently to incorporate, using the same gentle folding motions used for the brown butter. The folding should take 30-45 seconds. The potatoes will transform noticeably—they'll become creamier and lighter in texture as the liquid is folded in. Do not overmix even though the potatoes look stiff initially—another 10-15 seconds of gentle folding is sufficient. 1-2 minutes Cream and milk are fully incorporated, no streaks remain, potatoes are creamy and silky, mixture mounds slightly when scooped with a spoon but is not stiff
  9. 9 Add the remaining salt (approximately 1/4 teaspoon), white pepper, and freshly grated nutmeg. Fold these seasonings in gently using 8-10 folding motions. Taste the potatoes and assess the seasoning. Add a tiny pinch more salt if needed, folding it in. The potatoes should taste well-seasoned with salt enhancing the potato flavor, and the nutmeg should be barely perceptible as a flavor note (you taste the effect it has on creaminess rather than a distinct nutmeg flavor). Adjust seasoning to your preference. 1 minute Seasonings are incorporated, no visible spice specks remain, flavor is well-balanced and seasoned
  10. 10 Transfer the finished mashed potatoes to a warm serving bowl. The potatoes will continue to thicken slightly as they cool, so they should appear to be the perfect consistency at serving time (not stiff, but thicker than they are in the pot). Garnish with the fresh chopped chives and a small pinch of white pepper or nutmeg if desired. Serve immediately while the potatoes are still hot. 2-3 minutes Potatoes are in serving bowl, steam rises from them, chives are visible on top, texture appears creamy and silky

item.title: Butternut Squash Soup: Building Sweetness and Managing Starch Structure
item.description: Learn to make butternut squash soup that develops deep, natural sweetness through proper roasting and manages texture through careful starch gelatinization. Understand the science behind why your soup tastes flat and how to fix it. | ⏱️ Prep: 15min | Cook: 45min | Serves: 4 | #cat1761229889885 #easypost #1hourmeals #post #cooking
item.dc:creator: author_001
item.content:encoded:
⏱️ Prep Time: 15 minutes

🍳 Cook Time: 45 minutes

⏰ Total Time: 60 minutes

🍽️ Servings: 4

📊 Difficulty: Easy

🥘 Ingredients

  • 1 large butternut squash (approximately 3-3.5 lbs / 1.4-1.6kg), halved lengthwise and seeded
  • 3 tbsp (45ml) olive oil, divided
  • 1 medium yellow onion (about 250g), roughly chopped
  • 4 cloves (20g) garlic, sliced
  • 4 cups (960ml) vegetable or chicken broth, unsalted preferred
  • 3/4 cup (180ml) coconut milk (full-fat canned preferred)
  • 1 tsp (2g) fine sea salt, plus additional to taste
  • 1/4 tsp (0.5g) black pepper
  • 1/2 tsp (1g) ground nutmeg, freshly grated if possible
  • 1/4 tsp (0.5g) ground ginger, optional but recommended
  • Pinch (0.5g) cayenne pepper, optional for subtle heat
  • 1 tbsp (15ml) fresh lemon juice or apple cider vinegar, for balancing sweetness

👩‍🍳 Instructions

  1. 1 Preheat your oven to 425°F (220°C). While the oven heats, wash the butternut squash under running water and pat it dry with paper towels. Using a sharp knife, carefully cut the squash in half lengthwise. The squash is hard and the skin is tough, so use a chef's knife rather than a paring knife, and cut away from your body. Once halved, scoop out the seeds and stringy fiber using a sturdy spoon, creating a clean cavity. Discard the seeds or save them for roasting separately (they're edible when dried and salted). The cleaned halves are now ready for roasting. 5-10 minutes (mostly prep, oven preheating happens in parallel) Oven reaches 425°F and is preheating, squash halves are dry with no moisture on exterior, seed cavity is completely cleaned out, no fiber remaining inside
  2. 2 Drizzle 1.5 tablespoons (22ml) of olive oil into each squash cavity, then use your hands or a brush to coat the flesh evenly. The oil is essential for browning—without it, the squash steams rather than roasts. Season the inside of each half with approximately 1/4 teaspoon salt and a small pinch of black pepper. Place the halves cut-side-down on a baking sheet. The direct contact between the cut flesh and the hot baking sheet creates the browning that develops flavor. If the halves rock or don't lie flat, you can trim a thin strip from the rounded side, but usually they sit relatively flat. Place the baking sheet in the preheated oven. 5 minutes Oil is distributed on squash flesh, seasoning is visible on interior, squash halves are positioned cut-side-down on baking sheet, ready for oven
  3. 3 Roast the squash for 35-45 minutes. You want the flesh to become completely tender (a fork pierces it easily) and the edges where the flesh meets the skin to develop golden-brown coloration. The roasting time varies depending on squash size and oven variation. After 30 minutes, check by piercing the thickest part of the flesh with a fork. If the flesh still has resistance, return to the oven and check every 5 minutes. When fully roasted, the flesh will look slightly caramelized at the edges and feel completely soft. The entire kitchen will smell intensely sweet and slightly caramelized. 35-45 minutes Fork slides through flesh with no resistance, edges of flesh show golden-brown coloration, flesh pulls away from skin easily when pricked, kitchen smells of caramelized sweetness
  4. 4 Remove the squash from the oven and let it cool for 5-10 minutes until it's cool enough to handle but still warm. Using a sturdy spoon, scoop the roasted flesh from the skin. The flesh should come away from the skin easily in large pieces. You should have approximately 2.5-3 cups (600-720ml) of roasted squash flesh. Set the squash aside in a bowl and discard the skin. 10 minutes (includes cooling and scooping) Squash is cool enough to handle, flesh separates from skin easily, yielding approximately 2.5-3 cups of cooked flesh
  5. 5 While the squash roasts, prepare your aromatics. Roughly chop the onion into bite-sized pieces (they'll soften during cooking, so size isn't critical). Slice the garlic. Heat 1.5 tablespoons (22ml) of olive oil in a large pot over medium heat. Add the chopped onion and cook, stirring occasionally, for approximately 5 minutes until the onion becomes translucent and softened. Add the sliced garlic and cook for another minute until fragrant. You want the garlic to release its aroma without taking on color. If the garlic begins to brown, reduce the heat—garlic burned during this quick sauté will taste bitter in the final soup. 6-7 minutes Onion is translucent and softened, no raw onion texture remains, garlic releases strong aroma, no browning on garlic or onion
  6. 6 Add the roasted squash flesh to the pot with the cooked onion and garlic. Pour in the unsalted vegetable or chicken broth. The broth should come to approximately the same level as the squash mixture (a simmer at this stage isn't critical because you're about to blend everything). Increase the heat to medium-high and bring the mixture to a boil, then immediately reduce to medium and maintain a gentle simmer for approximately 10 minutes. This simmering allows flavors to begin melding and softens the remaining vegetable pieces if they aren't completely cooked. The simmering also gives you time to prepare your blender. 10-12 minutes Squash and aromatics are completely submerged in broth, mixture simmers gently with small bubbles breaking the surface, steam rises from pot
  7. 7 Working carefully with hot soup, blend in batches if using a food processor or blender. If using an immersion blender (recommended), place it directly in the pot and blend for 30-45 seconds until the soup reaches a completely smooth consistency. You're looking for a velvety texture with no visible squash chunks. Avoid over-blending (beyond 60 seconds), which can over-release starch and create a gluey, pasty texture. With an immersion blender, submerge the blade completely before turning it on to prevent splashing. If using a traditional blender, work in batches—fill the blender approximately halfway with hot soup, blend until smooth, then transfer to a clean pot and repeat with remaining soup. This prevents overflow and splashing of hot liquid. The entire blending process should be quick and efficient. 2-3 minutes total (30-45 seconds for immersion blender, or several minutes for traditional blender in batches) Soup reaches completely smooth consistency with no visible squash pieces, texture is velvety and pourable (not gluey or pasty)
  8. 8 Pour the coconut milk into the blended soup and stir thoroughly to incorporate. Add the nutmeg, ground ginger (if using), and a small pinch of cayenne pepper if you want subtle heat. Stir well to distribute spices evenly throughout. Do not heat the soup again at this point—coconut milk can separate if exposed to prolonged high heat. At this stage, taste the soup carefully. Add the lemon juice or apple cider vinegar, starting with 1 tablespoon (15ml) and adding more if needed. The acid should not make the soup taste sour; instead, it should make the other flavors taste brighter and more prominent. Add additional salt to taste—remember you salted the roasted squash earlier and the broth already contains salt (if unsalted broth was used, you may need 1-2 teaspoons total). 3-4 minutes Coconut milk is fully incorporated and soup has uniform color, spices are distributed evenly, acid is balanced (not sour-tasting), seasoning is adjusted to preference
  9. 9 Gently reheat the soup if it has cooled during blending. Place the pot over low heat and warm the soup to serving temperature (approximately 160-170°F / 71-77°C), stirring occasionally. This should take 3-5 minutes. Do not allow the soup to boil—coconut milk can separate and become grainy at high temperatures. The soup should be steaming gently but not actively bubbling. Serve immediately in warmed bowls. 3-5 minutes Soup steams gently, temperature is comfortable to eat (hot but not mouth-burning), coconut milk remains incorporated and smooth
  10. 10 Garnish each bowl with one or more of the following: a drizzle of coconut milk or cream in a thin line across the top, fresh thyme leaves, toasted pumpkin seeds, a small pinch of nutmeg, or crispy sage leaves. The garnish should be visually minimal but add textural contrast—the pumpkin seeds provide crunch, the herb adds freshness, the cream adds luxury. Serve immediately while the soup is hot. 2-3 minutes Garnish is added and bowl is ready for serving, soup is still steaming
